content.text: Microsoft Entra B2B (Business-to-Business) collaboration allows you to invite external identities — partners, vendors, contractors — into your tenant as guest users. Guests authenticate using their own home identity provider and receive a shadow object in your Entra ID directory with userType set to 'Guest'.
A guest user is not a new account — it is a cross-tenant reference. The guest authenticates at home and accesses resources in your tenant through that trusted reference.

When a guest redeems an invitation, Entra ID creates a user object in your tenant. The UPN follows the pattern external_domain.com#EXT#@yourtenant.onmicrosoft.com. Key object properties distinguish guests from member users.
Guests can authenticate using a wide range of home identity providers. The resource tenant does not need to manage their credentials — it only trusts their authentication.
Any email address, no IdP required

Invitations can be sent by admins, users with the Guest Inviter role, or any member (if the tenant allows it). The invited user receives an email and must redeem the invitation before they can access resources.

# Invite a single guest user
New-MgInvitation -InvitedUserEmailAddress 'partner@fabrikam.com' \
    -InviteRedirectUrl 'https://myapps.microsoft.com' \
    -InvitedUserDisplayName 'Alex Partner' \
    -SendInvitationMessage:$true

# Bulk invite from CSV: email,displayName
$guests = Import-Csv -Path ./guest-list.csv
foreach ($g in $guests) {
    New-MgInvitation \
        -InvitedUserEmailAddress $g.email \
        -InviteRedirectUrl 'https://myapps.microsoft.com' \
        -InvitedUserDisplayName $g.displayName \
        -SendInvitationMessage:$true
    Write-Host "Invited: $($g.email)"
}

For programmatic invitations in pipelines or external portals, use the invitations endpoint. This is also useful for customizing the invitation message or skipping email when the link is delivered out of band.

# Invite via Graph API (useful in automation pipelines)
$body = @{
    invitedUserEmailAddress = 'partner@fabrikam.com'
    inviteRedirectUrl = 'https://myapps.microsoft.com'
    invitedUserDisplayName = 'Alex Partner'
    sendInvitationMessage = $true
    invitedUserMessageInfo = @{
        messageLanguage = 'en-US'
        customizedMessageBody = 'Welcome to Contoso collaboration portal.'
    }
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 3

$result = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/invitations' \
    -Body $body -ContentType 'application/json'

Write-Host "Invite status: $($result.status)"
Write-Host "Invite URL: $($result.inviteRedeemUrl)"

Cross-Tenant Access Settings (CTAS) let you control exactly which users from which partner tenants can access your resources — and whether to trust their MFA or device compliance claims. Configure per-partner trust instead of relying on global defaults.

Guests follow the same Conditional Access framework as members. A dedicated policy targeting B2B guest users should require MFA at minimum. Avoid requiring compliant devices unless you provision and manage the guest device via Intune.
Guest accounts accumulate over time and become orphaned when the business relationship ends. Quarterly access reviews with auto-deny on no-response keep the guest population clean and auditable.
Use KQL to identify guests who have not signed in for 90 or more days. These accounts are candidates for removal or an access review follow-up.

// Find guest users who have not signed in for 90+ days
let cutoff = ago(90d);
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1d)
| where Category == 'UserManagement'
| where OperationName == 'Add user'
| where TargetResources[0].userPrincipalName contains '#EXT#'
| project GuestUPN = tostring(TargetResources[0].userPrincipalName),
          InvitedAt = TimeGenerated
| join kind=leftouter (
    SigninLogs
    | where UserType == 'Guest'
    | summarize LastSignIn = max(TimeGenerated) by UserPrincipalName
) on $left.GuestUPN == $right.UserPrincipalName
| where isnull(LastSignIn) or LastSignIn < cutoff
| project GuestUPN, InvitedAt, LastSignIn
| order by InvitedAt asc

Automate stale guest removal with PowerShell. Use the signInActivity property (requires Entra ID P1 or P2) to filter on last sign-in date, then remove accounts that exceed the retention policy threshold.

By default, guests have limited access to the Entra ID directory — they cannot enumerate all users or groups. You can tighten or relax these defaults via External Identities settings or the Guest User Access Restrictions blade.
Direct Federation with Partner Identity Providers

Direct federation allows users from a specific external domain to sign in using their own SAML 2.0 or WS-Federation IdP, without needing a Microsoft account. This is the right choice for large partners who operate their own Active Directory Federation Services (ADFS) or other corporate IdPs.

# Configure SAML/WS-Fed direct federation with a partner IdP
# Requires the partner's SAML metadata URL or manual metadata input
$fedBody = @{
    '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.samlOrWsFedExternalDomainFederation'
    issuerUri = 'https://sts.fabrikam.com/adfs/services/trust'
    metadataExchangeUri = 'https://sts.fabrikam.com/adfs/services/trust/mex'
    signingCertificate = '<base64-encoded-cert>'
    passiveSignInUri = 'https://sts.fabrikam.com/adfs/ls/'
    preferredAuthenticationProtocol = 'saml'
    domains = @(
        @{ id = 'fabrikam.com' }
    )
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 4

Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directory/federationConfigurations' \
    -Body $fedBody -ContentType 'application/json'

Write-Host 'Direct federation configured - users from fabrikam.com will now authenticate via their ADFS'

Google federation enables B2B invitations to gmail.com and Google Workspace domains. Users sign in via Google OAuth rather than email OTP, providing a smoother experience. Configure via an OAuth 2.0 app in Google Cloud Console first.

# Enable Google federation for B2B guests with @gmail.com or @googleworkspace domains
# Prereq: Google Cloud OAuth app with client ID and secret
$googleFed = @{
    '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.socialIdentityProvider'
    displayName = 'Google'
    identityProviderType = 'Google'
    clientId = '123456789-abcdefgh.apps.googleusercontent.com'
    clientSecret = $env:GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET
} | ConvertTo-Json

Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identity/identityProviders' \
    -Body $googleFed -ContentType 'application/json'

# After enabling, users with Gmail addresses will use Google as their IdP
# and will NOT receive email OTP prompts

Entitlement Management's Access Packages let you bundle resources (SharePoint, Teams, apps, groups) and give external users a self-service request portal. Packages expire automatically and integrate with access reviews, making them preferable to manual group assignments for long-running collaboration.

# Create an Access Package for external partners using Entitlement Management
# Step 1: create a catalog
$catalog = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityGovernance/entitlementManagement/accessPackageCatalogs' \
    -Body (@{ displayName = 'Partner Resources'; description = 'Resources for B2B partners'; isExternallyVisible = $true } | ConvertTo-Json) \
    -ContentType 'application/json'

# Step 2: create the access package
$pkg = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityGovernance/entitlementManagement/accessPackages' \
    -Body (@{ displayName = 'Partner Collaboration Package'; catalogId = $catalog.id; isHidden = $false } | ConvertTo-Json) \
    -ContentType 'application/json'

# Step 3: add an assignment policy allowing external users to request
$policyBody = @{
    accessPackageId = $pkg.id
    displayName = 'External Partner Request Policy'
    allowedTargetScope = 'specificDirectoryUsersByPolicy'
    specificAllowedTargets = @(@{ '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.connectedOrganizationMembers'; description = 'All partner orgs'; isBackup = $false })
    expiration = @{ duration = 'P365D'; type = 'afterDuration' }
    requestApprovalSettings = @{ isApprovalRequiredForAdd = $true }
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 5

Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityGovernance/entitlementManagement/assignmentPolicies' \
    -Body $policyBody -ContentType 'application/json'

Write-Host "Access package created: $($pkg.displayName) (id: $($pkg.id))"

Sign-in Logs and Audit Logs in Log Analytics provide visibility into how guests are using your resources, where they are signing in from, and which invitations were never redeemed. Use these queries to drive lifecycle management decisions.

// Summarise guest user sign-in activity over the past 30 days
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where UserType == 'Guest'
| summarize
    SignInCount    = count(),
    SuccessCount   = countif(ResultType == 0),
    FailureCount   = countif(ResultType != 0),
    AppCount       = dcount(AppDisplayName),
    LastSignIn     = max(TimeGenerated)
    by UserPrincipalName, UserDisplayName
| extend SuccessRate = round(100.0 * SuccessCount / SignInCount, 1)
| order by SignInCount desc

// Find guests signing in from unexpected countries
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where UserType == 'Guest'
| where Location !in ('US','GB','CA','DE','FR','AU')  // adjust to your expected regions
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, Location, AppDisplayName, ResultType
| order by TimeGenerated desc

Audit Logs record every invite, redemption, and deletion event for guest users. Build a daily summary and alert on unredeemed invitations — these represent open access that was never used and should be cancelled to reduce attack surface.

// Track guest invitation and redemption events
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where OperationName in (
    'Invite external user',
    'Redeem external user invite',
    'Delete user'
)
| extend
    GuestEmail  = tostring(TargetResources[0].userPrincipalName),
    InvitedBy   = tostring(InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName),
    Result      = tostring(Result)
| summarize
    count() by bin(TimeGenerated, 1d), OperationName, Result
| order by TimeGenerated desc

// Alert: guest invited and never redeemed within 14 days
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated between (ago(30d) .. ago(14d))
| where OperationName == 'Invite external user'
| extend GuestEmail = tostring(TargetResources[0].userPrincipalName)
| join kind=leftanti (
    AuditLogs
    | where OperationName == 'Redeem external user invite'
    | extend GuestEmail = tostring(TargetResources[0].userPrincipalName)
) on GuestEmail
| project GuestEmail, InvitedAt = TimeGenerated
| order by InvitedAt asc
Guests can sign in with password only
Disable guest inviting in External Identities settings
Guests can enumerate all users and groups
Restrict via Guest User Access Restrictions
Set invitation expiry (default 30 days in portal)
B2B guests authenticate at their home tenant and access your resources via a shadow object in your directory.
The guest UPN follows the external_domain.com#EXT#@tenant.onmicrosoft.com pattern with userType=Guest.
Supported identity providers include Entra ID, MSA, Google, Facebook, SAML/WS-Fed, and email OTP.
Cross-Tenant Access Settings let you trust partner MFA and control which users from which tenants can collaborate.
Conditional Access policies should target B2B guests explicitly and require MFA.
Quarterly access reviews with auto-deny keep the guest population auditable and remove orphaned accounts.
Use signInActivity filtering to identify and remove stale guests who have not signed in for 90+ days.
